entities.name: Thomas Aquinas
entities.description: A theologian/doctor of the church who provides arguments for the legality of conjurations and charmes.

entities.name: A person who denied the legality of conjuration in a specific incident involving monks.
Anthotiius Prafensis
entities.description: cardinal and legat for the pope throughout France.

entities.name: A saint used to support arguments regarding the power of God's name vs relics.
M. Mai.
entities.description: A source cited by Scot to discuss the legality of exorcism without conjuration.

entities.name: St. Jerome/S. Jerome (not explicitly named but implied in context)
John Wierus
entities.description: A person whose writings on conjurations are mentioned by Bodin.

entities.name: Jean Bodin
entities.description: Author/thinker who discusses the punishment of witches vs conjurors.

entities.name: Raphaell
entities.description: Archangel mentioned in relation to the liver of fish.

entities.name: Peter or Barbara
entities.description: Names used in a specific conjuration formula for the sick.

entities.name: conjuration of salt
entities.description: A ritual involving the consecration and conjuration of salt for health.

entities.name: exorcism of incense
entities.description: A ritual to drive away spirits from frankincense.

entities.name: the devil
entities.description: The primary antagonist in the rituals and discussions of exorcism.

summary: This passage from Reginald Scot's 'Discoverie of Witchcraft' details various popish conjurations, exorcisms, and rituals involving materials like salt, incense, and water. It also includes theological arguments regarding the legality of and effectiveness of the power of God versus the/the devil in ritual practice.
